query: Do the Arabia Terra deposits record only one fluvial style or a diverse range of depositional systems?

candidates.title: MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1 (directory walk + probe): HRSC digital terrain models (DTMs) provide the topographic context needed to distinguish different fluvial depositional systems in Arabia Terra (e.g., channelized deposits vs fan/delta/lacustrine basins) via slopes, basin geometry, and paleoflow routing.

candidates.title: MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Map-projected HRSC reflectance mosaics/images support regional-scale geomorphic mapping across Arabia Terra (identifying channel patterns, distributary networks, fan/delta morphologies, and relationships among layered deposits).

candidates.title: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER PEDR AND EGDR ARCHIVES
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: MOLA Mission Experiment Gridded Data Records (global gridded topography) are fundamental for basin-scale assessment of whether Arabia Terra deposits reflect a single fluvial style or multiple systems (integrated drainage, base-level/lake basin identification, regional slopes).

candidates.title: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER IEGDR ARCHIVE
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: MOLA IEGDR gridded products complement MEGDR for detailed topographic analyses and cross-checking elevation/derived surfaces used in depositional-system interpretation.

candidates.title: MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: CRISM MRDR (map-projected reduced data record) multispectral/hyperspectral cubes enable compositional mapping (clays/sulfates/oxides) in Arabia Terra deposits, helping distinguish fluvial/alluvial vs lacustrine vs altered volcanic/aeolian components.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MRDR Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: PDS4 collection-level equivalent of CRISM MRDR map-projected products; preferred modern identifier for compositional mapping of depositional units in Arabia Terra.

candidates.title: MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: CRISM targeted TRDR/ADR reduced products provide higher-fidelity spectra for key localities within Arabia Terra where specific depositional interpretations (e.g., deltaic vs alluvial vs lacustrine) hinge on mineralogy.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM TRDR Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: PDS4 collection-level equivalent of CRISM TRDR targeted reduced data products (spectral cubes), useful for detailed mineralogical tests of multiple depositional environments within Arabia Terra.

candidates.title: MRO SHARAD OBSERVATIONS
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: SHARAD radargram products help evaluate internal layering/thickness/architecture of Arabia Terra deposits, which can discriminate between different depositional styles (e.g., repeated aggradation events, buried channels, lacustrine packages).

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter SHARAD U.S. Radargrams Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: PDS4 collection-level identifier for SHARAD U.S. team radargrams; modern access point for subsurface stratigraphic context relevant to interpreting depositional-system diversity in Arabia Terra.

candidates.title: MARS GLOBAL SURVEYOR TES-DERIVED THERMAL INERTIA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: TES-derived thermal inertia maps provide particle-size/induration proxies that can help separate depositional facies (e.g., indurated channel fills vs finer-grained basin deposits) when assessing whether Arabia Terra records multiple fluvial styles.

tool_input.summary: Arabia Terra deposits are unlikely to be explained by only a single fluvial style; testing for a *diverse range* of depositional systems typically requires combining (1) topography (MOLA, HRSC DTMs) to map drainage/basin context and (2) surface composition/physical-property constraints (CRISM mineralogy; TES thermal inertia), with (3) optional subsurface stratigraphic context (SHARAD radargrams). Stage 1 GEO-node directory-walk located the datasets above as the closest supporting PDS holdings for this question.

parts.content:
You are a dataset-discovery assistant for the NASA PDS Geosciences (GEO) node at https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/

Directory layout and workflow:
Directory layout: mission/ → dataset_or_bundle/ → volume/ (PDS3) or sub-collections (PDS4)
This node has a mission layer. Start with pds_list_missions(node='geo') or, if you already know the mission directory from the abbreviation table, skip directly to pds_list_dataset_dirs(path='<mission>/', node='geo').
Most queries can be answered in 3 tool calls: list_dataset_dirs → probe_datasets → inspect_collections.


Common mission/instrument abbreviations:
  Mars Express = MEX → mex/ (instruments: HRSC, OMEGA, MARSIS, PFS, SPICAM, MaRS)
  Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter = MRO → mro/ (instruments: HiRISE, CTX, CRISM, SHARAD, MCS)
  Mars Science Laboratory / Curiosity = MSL → msl/ (instruments: ChemCam, APXS, CheMin, SAM, Mastcam, MAHLI, DAN)
  Mars 2020 / Perseverance = M2020 → m2020/ (instruments: PIXL, SHERLOC, Mastcam-Z, SuperCam, RIMFAX)
  Mars Exploration Rovers (Spirit=MER2, Opportunity=MER1) → mer/ (instruments: Pancam, Mini-TES/MTES, APXS, MB, MI)
  Mars Global Surveyor = MGS → mgs/ (instruments: MOC, MOLA, TES, MAG)
  Mars Odyssey = ODY → ody/ (instruments: THEMIS, GRS, NS)
  Phoenix = PHX → phx/ (instruments: TEGA, MECA, SSI, OM, RAC)
  Viking = VL1/VL2/VO1/VO2 → viking/ (instruments: camera, IRTM, MAWD)
  MESSENGER = MESS → messenger/ (instruments: MDIS, GRNS, XRS, MLA, MASCS)
  Magellan = MGN → mgn/ (instruments: SAR, altimetry, radiometry, emissivity)
  LRO → lro/ (instruments: LOLA, Diviner, LROC, Mini-RF, LAMP)
  GRAIL → grail/ (instruments: LGRS)
  NEAR → near/ (instruments: NLR, MSI, XGRS, MAG)
  InSight → insight/ (instruments: SEIS, HP3, RISE, IDA)


PDS3 vs PDS4:
  - PDS3 directory names are ALL-CAPS-style hyphenated identifiers (e.g. mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1). Leaf marker: voldesc.cat or voldesc.sfd.
  - PDS4 bundle directories begin with `urn-nasa-pds-`. Leaf marker: bundle*.xml or bundle*.lblx. Collections live in subdirectories.
  - Hybrid: some directories have BOTH PDS3 and PDS4 labels.

YOUR TOOLS (5 tools — always pass node='geo'):
  1. pds_list_missions(node='geo') — returns mission directories with descriptions. No HTTP.
  2. pds_list_dataset_dirs(path, node='geo', filter=...) — lists sub-directory names under a path. Cheap HTTP, no label parsing.
  3. pds_probe_datasets(paths, node='geo') — probes specific directories for PDS labels. Accepts a list of paths (max 20).
  4. pds_inspect_collections(path, node='geo') — scans PDS4 bundle subdirs for collection labels.
  5. pds_resolve_volume(volume_set_path, node='geo', dataset_id_hint=..., sample=8) — when a parent directory contains many
     numbered sibling volumes (RMS COISS_2xxx, COUVIS_0xxx, ATM
     MROM_*, jnomwr_*, cocirs_*, IMG mrox_*, MSGRMDS_*), this probes
     hint-ranked children in one call and returns per-child
     dataset_ids plus a `best_match` field. Use it instead of
     probing volumes one at a time when you know the target id or
     a discriminating substring of it.

WORKFLOW:
Step 1: If you know the mission directory from the abbreviation table, skip directly to list_dataset_dirs(path='<mission>/', node='geo').
        Otherwise call pds_list_missions(node='geo') first.
Step 2: Call pds_list_dataset_dirs for the relevant mission directory. Scan names and pds_hints to identify promising datasets.
Step 3: Call pds_probe_datasets with the most relevant paths (batch up to 20).
Step 4: If PDS4 bundles are found, call pds_inspect_collections on top 2-3.
Step 5: Return candidates.
Most queries can be answered in 3 tool calls: list_dataset_dirs → probe_datasets → inspect_collections.

CRITICAL RULES:
  1. EVERY query requires at least one candidate. Never dismiss a query as 'not a dataset request' — every paper-derived query has at least one
     supporting PDS dataset somewhere in this node's archive, and your job
     is to surface the closest match (with `reasoning` explaining the link).
  2. Prefer CALIBRATED/REDUCED data over raw/EDR for science queries.
     When a probed voldesc exposes `dataset_ids` with multiple entries,
     match the question's data need to the PRODUCT-TYPE token inside each
     id (typically the third or fourth dash-separated token). Common ones:
         EDR / RAW     = raw observations
         CDR / CALIB   = generic calibrated data
         RDR / TRDR / MRDR / MTRDR / BDR / SCVDR = reduced / derived imagery or spectra
         DDR / LDR     = derived / digital terrain / level-5 products
         SSB           = solar / stellar occultation (Cassini UVIS)
         SPEC / CUBE / WAV = spectra / spectral cubes / waveforms
         QUBE          = VIMS spectral data cube (the VIMS-flavoured "EDR")
     If the query mentions 'occultation' return the SSB id; 'spectra' →
     SPEC; 'calibrated radiance' → CALIB/CDR; 'mosaic / map / derived' →
     DDR/MRDR/MTRDR. When two product types both fit the question (e.g.
     SPEC + SSB for an occultation spectra query), return both candidates.
     Do NOT just return the scalar `dataset_id` and drop the rest — that
     field is only the first id in the voldesc and is often the wrong
     product type for a given query.
  3. For PDS4, use inspect_collections ONLY for top 2-3 bundles. Return COLLECTION-level LID when :data or :calibrated exists.
  4. When the SAME data is available as both PDS3 and PDS4, return BOTH identifiers — emit one candidate per identifier. Most published papers cite the PDS3 dataset_id (e.g. MESS-E_V_H_SW-MAG-3-CDR-CALIBRATED-V1.0, LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0); the PDS4 collection LID is the modern equivalent. Do NOT silently drop the PDS3 form.
  5. Stay under 20 tool calls per query. Soft cap: 15. Hard cap: 20.
  6. Only return paths you've actually probed — never guess.
  7. ANTI-THRASHING. Never re-issue a tool call with the same name and the
     same key arguments (path, paths, filter) that you've already issued in
     this trace. The result will be identical — it wastes the budget and
     blocks progress. If you're tempted to re-probe the same set of paths,
     either move to a different tool (inspect_collections, resolve_volume)
     or commit to `final_result` with the best candidate you have so far.
  8. STOP-AT-LEVEL. The output `path` field should point at a dataset or
     bundle directory (containing voldesc.cat or bundle*.xml), NOT at a
     deeper product / DATA / EXTRAS / BROWSE / SHAPEFILE / DTM sub-folder.
     Feature-level retrieval (a specific image of a specific crater) is
     out of scope for this finder; return the dataset and explain in
     `reasoning` how it covers the feature.
  9. After 15 tool calls without converging, ALWAYS prefer emitting your
     current best candidate(s) over running another exploratory call.
     A grounded near-match with a clear reasoning note is more useful
     than a 20-call trace that times out with no `final_result`.

===========================================================
STAGE 2 — DEEPER NODE-SPECIFIC SEARCH (escalation only)
===========================================================

Everything above is STAGE 1: the 5 live-HTTP tools (pds_list_missions / pds_list_dataset_dirs / pds_probe_datasets / pds_inspect_collections / pds_resolve_volume), the per-node workflow, and the Critical Rules ALL belong to Stage 1. Stage 1 reaches bundle/collection level by walking the node's directory tree. Stage 2 below is a SECOND set of tools for refining or rescuing Stage 1 — do NOT call any Stage 2 tool unless one of the escalation triggers below fires.

WHEN TO ESCALATE TO STAGE 2 (any one is enough):
  (a) The query asks for SPECIFIC observations, granules, or product IDs — not just a dataset. Example: 'Cassini ISS images of Mimas between 2009 and 2010' needs per-observation filtering.
  (b) Stage 1 has located the right bundle/collection but you still need to narrow by GEOMETRY, TIME, or a SPATIAL filter (bbox, lat/lon, ring longitude, target body, observation type).
  (c) Stage 1 produced AMBIGUOUS candidates that a faceted query can disambiguate (e.g. multiple instruments with similar names, multiple processing levels).
  (d) Stage 1 cannot reach the node's holdings index (e.g. SBN returns HTTP 403, a directory listing is unavailable). Use the Stage 2 API as a fallback path to candidates.

STAGE 2 TOOLS (this node):
  - ode_search_products_tool(target=..., instrument=..., bbox=..., pt=..., product_type=...): faceted product search across GEO/ODE holdings. Filter by target body, instrument, bounding box, lat/lon point, or product type.
  - ode_count_products_tool(target=..., instrument=..., bbox=...): cheap count before pulling a large result set.
  - ode_list_instruments_tool(target=...): enumerate instrument codes valid for a target.
  - ode_list_feature_classes_tool(target=...) / ode_list_feature_names_tool(target=..., feature_class=...): gazetteer lookup for named surface features (craters, maria, etc.).
  - ode_get_feature_bounds_tool(target=..., feature_name=...): bounding box for a named feature; feed the bbox into ode_search_products_tool.

STAGE 2 RULES:
  1. When you already have a bundle/collection LID from Stage 1, REUSE it as a filter in the Stage 2 call. Stage 2 is refinement, not a fresh search.
  2. Spend the MINIMUM number of additional calls. If a single faceted call answers the question, stop.
  3. If Stage 2 finds nothing or errors out, FALL BACK to the Stage 1 candidates and note in `reasoning` that Stage 2 was attempted and returned no refinement.
  4. The 20-tool-call budget from Stage 1's Critical Rule 5 is the budget for BOTH stages combined. Do NOT reset the counter when entering Stage 2.
  5. Every candidate's `reasoning` field must explicitly name which stage produced it (e.g. 'Stage 1: located bundle X by directory walk' or 'Stage 2: narrowed to product Y via ODE_search_products bbox filter').
Do the Arabia Terra deposits record only one fluvial style or a diverse range of depositional systems?

VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains targeted reduced data record (TRDR) and ancillary data record (ADR) data acquired by the Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter Compact Reconnaissance Imaging Spectrometer for Mars (CRISM). The volume also contains detailed documentation about the mission, instrument, and data set, as well as an index table.

results.title: MARS GLOBAL SURVEYOR TES-TSDR

VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains Mars Global Surveyor TES TSDR standard data products, along with documentation and other ancillary information about the data products. This is a re-release of this data within this dataset. The release is justified by the improvement of data in at least one table of this volume: ATM, BOL, GEO, OBS, POS, and/or RAD.

parts.args: {"candidates":[{"path":"mex/mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1/mexhrs_2001/","dataset_id":"MEX-M-HRSC-5-REFDR-DTM-V1.0","title":"MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mex","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1 (directory walk + probe): HRSC digital terrain models (DTMs) provide the topographic context needed to distinguish different fluvial depositional systems in Arabia Terra (e.g., channelized deposits vs fan/delta/lacustrine basins) via slopes, basin geometry, and paleoflow routing."},{"path":"mex/mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-mapprojected-v3/mexhrs_1003/","dataset_id":"MEX-M-HRSC-5-REFDR-MAPPROJECTED-V3.0","title":"MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mex","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: Map-projected HRSC reflectance mosaics/images support regional-scale geomorphic mapping across Arabia Terra (identifying channel patterns, distributary networks, fan/delta morphologies, and relationships among layered deposits)."},{"path":"mgs/mgs-m-mola-5-megdr-l3-v1/mgsl_300x/","dataset_id":"MGS-M-MOLA-5-MEGDR-L3-V1.0","title":"MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER PEDR AND EGDR ARCHIVES","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mgs","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: MOLA Mission Experiment Gridded Data Records (global gridded topography) are fundamental for basin-scale assessment of whether Arabia Terra deposits reflect a single fluvial style or multiple systems (integrated drainage, base-level/lake basin identification, regional slopes)."},{"path":"mgs/mgs-m-mola-5-iegdr-l3-v2/mgsl_2xxx/","dataset_id":"MGS-M-MOLA-5-IEGDR-L3-V2.0","title":"MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER IEGDR ARCHIVE","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mgs","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: MOLA IEGDR gridded products complement MEGDR for detailed topographic analyses and cross-checking elevation/derived surfaces used in depositional-system interpretation."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-multispectral-v1/mrocr_3201/","dataset_id":"MRO-M-CRISM-5-RDR-MULTISPECTRAL-V1.0","title":"MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: CRISM MRDR (map-projected reduced data record) multispectral/hyperspectral cubes enable compositional mapping (clays/sulfates/oxides) in Arabia Terra deposits, helping distinguish fluvial/alluvial vs lacustrine vs altered volcanic/aeolian components."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-multispectral-v1/mrocr_3201/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:mro_crism_mrdr:data_mrdr","title":"Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MRDR Data Collection","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: PDS4 collection-level equivalent of CRISM MRDR map-projected products; preferred modern identifier for compositional mapping of depositional units in Arabia Terra."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-crism-3-rdr-targeted-v1/mrocr_2101/","dataset_id":"MRO-M-CRISM-3-RDR-TARGETED-V1.0","title":"MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: CRISM targeted TRDR/ADR reduced products provide higher-fidelity spectra for key localities within Arabia Terra where specific depositional interpretations (e.g., deltaic vs alluvial vs lacustrine) hinge on mineralogy."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-crism-3-rdr-targeted-v1/mrocr_2101/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:mro_crism_trdr_2101:data_trdr","title":"Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM TRDR Data Collection","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: PDS4 collection-level equivalent of CRISM TRDR targeted reduced data products (spectral cubes), useful for detailed mineralogical tests of multiple depositional environments within Arabia Terra."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-sharad-5-radargram-v2/mrosh_2101/","dataset_id":"MRO-M-SHARAD-5-RADARGRAM-V2.0","title":"MRO SHARAD OBSERVATIONS","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: SHARAD radargram products help evaluate internal layering/thickness/architecture of Arabia Terra deposits, which can discriminate between different depositional styles (e.g., repeated aggradation events, buried channels, lacustrine packages)."},{"path":"mro/mro-m-sharad-5-radargram-v2/mrosh_2101/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:mro_sharad_us_radargrams:data","title":"Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter SHARAD U.S. Radargrams Data Collection","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":"mro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: PDS4 collection-level identifier for SHARAD U.S. team radargrams; modern access point for subsurface stratigraphic context relevant to interpreting depositional-system diversity in Arabia Terra."},{"path":"mgs/mgs-m-tes-5-timap-v1/mgst_9001/","dataset_id":"MGS-M-TES-5-TIMAP-V1.0","title":"MARS GLOBAL SURVEYOR TES-DERIVED THERMAL INERTIA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"mgs","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: TES-derived thermal inertia maps provide particle-size/induration proxies that can help separate depositional facies (e.g., indurated channel fills vs finer-grained basin deposits) when assessing whether Arabia Terra records multiple fluvial styles."}],"summary":"Arabia Terra deposits are unlikely to be explained by only a single fluvial style; testing for a *diverse range* of depositional systems typically requires combining (1) topography (MOLA, HRSC DTMs) to map drainage/basin context and (2) surface composition/physical-property constraints (CRISM mineralogy; TES thermal inertia), with (3) optional subsurface stratigraphic context (SHARAD radargrams). Stage 1 GEO-node directory-walk located the datasets above as the closest supporting PDS holdings for this question."}
